summary: Todd Robinson, a syndication attorney, discusses legal structures for raising capital in multifamily real estate, including SEC compliance (506b/506c offerings), PPM documentation, and deal structuring. While focused on capital-raising mechanics, the conversation includes broader market observations about 2025-2026 multifamily conditions but lacks Brian's specific deal sourcing and underwriting challenges.
takeaways: 506b offerings allow up to 35 unaccredited investors with pre-existing substantive relationships (three-touch rule: met in person, had coffee, sent email); 506c offerings require accreditation but allow advertising
Attorney-drafted contracts (not broker contracts) protect buyers on reps/warranties, rent-ready credits (make-ready units), and collection contingencies before closing
Never accept broker contracts; insist on attorney-drafted contracts with minimum 3-page addendum covering conditions, reps/warranties, and rent-ready credits for vacant units
Tie rent rolls to bank deposit statements for minimum 6 months; request delinquency and eviction lists to verify stated economic occupancy (collections, not physical occupancy)
Syndication attorney fees: $12,500 flat fee for PPM/SEC filings; $15,000-$25,000 for transactional work on 15-50 unit deals; larger deals ($50M+) run $50,000-$80,000 with opinion letters
Property management: Keep units vacant rather than lease bad tenants; vacancy + turnover costs ($2-3K per unit) + eviction process + loss rent = worse than 1-2 month vacancy
To raise capital: start with friends/family (print LinkedIn/Facebook contacts), host dinner parties to establish pre-existing relationships for 506b compliance, avoid pitching directly

By 2026, supply/demand imbalance will compress as construction stops; rents will rebound but interest rates likely to stay 5-7% (normal), requiring sponsors to buy right and create value on revenue side

skill_candidates.change: Verify actual collections via bank deposits rather than relying on T12/rent roll, with minimum 6-month statement requirement
skill_candidates.evidence: Todd: '$25M deals have terminated because sellers won't provide bank statements...tie deposits to rent roll...a lot of sellers will push back...this is a red flag for me' and 'rent rolls are fudged T12 are fudged'

skill_candidates.change: Request delinquency list and eviction filing list and cross-match to identify true economic occupancy (not physical occupancy)
skill_candidates.evidence: Todd: 'match all that stuff up...when you start tying that in then you have recourse...rent rolls are cute but you show me your collections...difference between physical occupancy and economic occupancy'

skill_candidates.change: Require rent-ready/make-ready credits in contract for vacant units pre-closing; typical threshold is 10+ vacant units triggering $2K per-unit credit
skill_candidates.evidence: Todd: 'if there's 10 vacant units and they're going to cost $2,000 to turn...buyer gets a credit for those units...usually not found in broker contract...something you'll need to see in a [custom] agreement'

skill_candidates.change: Host relationship-building dinner parties as indirect networking to establish pre-existing substantive relationships (506b compliance) rather than direct investor pitches
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin: 'she hosted a party...called like influence influencers and connectors...she just invited everyone...not pitching a deal...deepening relationships...she probably had 50 people' and Todd: 'personal invitation personal relationship I would argue that is establishing a pre-existing substantive relation 100%'

title: How To Raise Money For Multifamily Real Estate Deals | For Beginners
